It is interesting that the LEDs still produce some current even though the phosphor is in the way between your light source and the chip itself. Maybe some blue light actually still gets through to the chip.

author: @johnrouse3061
text: This way of thinking... that of understanding the basic mechanics/ physics involved in systems is crucial to engineering; particularly DIY backyard inventor style!  I tend to think in these terms:  Almost all systems that produce "work," involve separating two forces (be they electrical or mechanical) and then when the "charges" or forces want to balance, you plug some type of machinery in to capture that; and voila!   I've contemplated "batteries" that are just big ol' weights that are lifted up, and as they fall, they generate a current by spinning or passing coils etc.  Or; one could pressurize metal tanks with air or a fluid (or vice versa,  pull a vacuum on the other side,); other ideas involve dissimilar metals, and applying heat (could be from a fresnel lens in the sun perhaps) on one side, and cooling the other (perhaps a cooling system akin to the old "swamp" cooling units/ air conditioners; Ie: water runs down fins with air over them etc.)...   I'm rambling a bit; but the point is, THERE ARE ENERGY/ POTENTIAL DIFFERENCES ALL AROUND US... ALL THAT MUST BE DONE IS TO ENGINEER MACHINERY AND SYSTEMS THAT EXPLOIT/ MAGNIFY, AND CAPTURE THEM TO DO USEFUL WORK... THERE'S INFINITE WAYS TO ACCOMPLISH THIS WITH A BIT OF INGENUITY, AND OLD FASHIONED CLEVER TINKERING!!! :)   Thanks so much Robert for sharing your experiments.  May we all inspire one another toward energy independence; which will, in turn, bring a more free and autonomous society at large.

author: @gridfreedomquest8613
text: You are showing the basics of how a solar or sun tracker works. The put 2 on an east plain and 2 leds on a north south plain.  They compare the power coming out of the east west diodes to each other and move the time that needs to point at the sun until the power output of each diode is the same.  It does the same for north south and when the power is equal the array should be in the spot where the suns power is the strongest.

author: @stevecummins324
text:
Aiming a pair of Leds at each other, inside a straw, using one as a led and the other in PV mode, is an old bodge for providing optical voltage isolation of a signal. Need to series duplicate the output for  higher output voltages.

Works pretty well for interfacing mosfet switches to arduinos. As it works like a voltage controlled voltage source... Connect the PV diodes between gate and source... And the gate-source voltage will never exceeds what the PV diodes can output. Allows n-channel mosfets to used as high or low side switches.

So... 
4mosfets... 2*2*4 diodes(led&pv doubled for voltage, for each mosfet) and that's the power side of a H-Bridge. 
Throw in an arduino for sequencing of the  control signals for the mosfets and that's pretty much a motor controller. Arduino has enough processing power to read say a variable resistor, or be set via digital coms from say an i2c bus.

Mosfets that can handle 50V and 50amp are pretty darn cheep these days.

author: @05Matz
text: If I remember right, LEDs can only absorb photons in the colour they emit or higher frequency. As white LEDs emit blue (and are covered in a phosphor that turns some fraction of blue light into other colours), they're unlikely to be very efficient capturing most light sources, but the blue and UV light from sunlight will likely create some power (if the UV isn't blocked by whatever material the lens is made of). Solar panels are essentially arrays of huge, far-IR LEDs (I guess photodiodes, technically, since they're designed specifically to minimize the amount of power they waste by emitting light) designed to maximise the range of light they're able to 'work with' and the surface area they cover. If you could find similar sources of red or IR LEDs, they would probably work much better than the white/blue ones. (but of course, they're much less common in scrap, particularly as big COB panels.)

author: @TerryGilsenan
text:
Excellent work. I wonder if using the COB's you would need to try and remove the phosphor coating? it would be acting as a wavelength filter and reducing the actual wavelengths reaching the silicon. I suspect you might see an improvement of up to 50% by simply removing the phosphor.

I am working on my own rural solar light project for remote sections of Papua New Guinea. I am using a single PV cell that has a peak voltage of around 0.6v and max current of around 5 amps. I am using a joule thief circuit to charge a single AA sized NiMH cell, and then I am using another joule thief to drive 5 x 3W LED (cree) modules. This is providing a full charge to the cell in about 5 hours, and will charge even in full shade. the fully charged cell can drive the 5 x 3w LED array for about 12 hours.

Sure the LED's are being under-driven, however they are indeed producing useable light, and they are not heating the junction and wasting energy as heat.

Your channel is an amazing resource.

Thank you.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: wow  - thank you ate and I like the idea of removing the phosphor

replies.author: @AndyFletcherX31
replies.text: I really like the idea of using a joule thief with a single cell to boost it to charge the battery. How much current are you getting from the joule thief and do you have problems starting it with only 0.6V on the input?

replies.author: @TerryGilsenan
replies.text:
@AndyFletcherX31 I haven't measured the current, I have been monitoring the voltage. I am getting a peak charging voltage of around 2v, and the internal resistance of the cell increases as it approaches full charge and the voltage from the charging joule thief drops to 1.45v when fully charged.

I will be doing other tests this week which will include current monitoring in the charger circuit, and the delivery circuit.

I have this connected to another joule thief circuit that works very well as a driver for 5 x 12v 3w cree elements. 

I have found that I am getting an open circuit voltage on the charger joule thief of around 20v with the PV cell in full shade and delivering only 0.3v. In full sun with the PV cell delivering 0.61v I am getting around 40v open circuit.

author: @TheIrishdriven
text: Mr Murray, i have often wondered if we can use  thermal couple or thermister tech to generate current like a solar panel but from heat ? then possibly capture the heat from appliances and return to a battery in the appliance with an inverter to reduce the consumption of grid power needed .? im unable to create a prototype at this time

replies.author: @TerryGilsenan
replies.text: This does actually work when coupling the TEC (peltier unit) with a joule thief circuit. You can charge a capacitor or battery and then use the energy from there.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Terry has given a really good answer mate

replies.author: @hanslepoeter5167
replies.text:
@TerryGilsenan Indeed. It's called a peltier element and is a big thermocouple. It will function in reverse.
A thermistor or any other resistor functionality does not work the other way around. The second law of thermodynamics forbids it. You can heat up an ntc or any other resistor whatever you want. It will never generate current.

replies.text: @garethb5729 If I remember correctly what Rob said in his video, the flat line lenticular sheets just redirect the light coming through it, they don't focus it like the round lenses that you'd use for a solar still/oven.  But, there's only one way to find out for sure!
